ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for porcini cream pasta; closest_parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for cream-sauced porcini pasta.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 'Pasta (food)' is the closest parent concept; no specific concept for porcini cream pasta exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of porcini cream pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of cooked pasta coated in a rich porcini mushroom cream sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with cream sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20420 and composite cream sauce reference)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for porcini cream pasta exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked enriched pasta (NDB 20420, ~158 kcal/100g cooked) with a cream-based mushroom sauce component. The composite reflects approximately 55% cooked pasta and 45% cream sauce by weight, yielding the per-100g values below. Porcini mushrooms contribute negligible macronutrient shift at typical rehydrated quantities. This is a best-available estimation from approved USDA sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta, cooked (white, boiled al dente) GI ~45
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for porcini cream pasta. GI of 45 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white pasta cooked al dente (GI ~45, low category). The cream sauce and porcini mushrooms do not substantially raise the GI; fat and protein in the sauce may modestly lower the effective GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 77.0) / 100 = 34.7, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.0 × 3.5 = 77.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: Porcini cream pasta contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (735 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of pasta is low (~45), a standard 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 77 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~35, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and butter base contributes approximately 5.8 g saturated fat per 100 g (~20 g per serving), exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g saturated fat for a 2,000 kcal diet in a single meal; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions or request a lighter sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Porcini cream pasta is an Italian pasta dish featuring cooked wheat pasta — typically tagliatelle, fettuccine, or pappardelle — tossed in a rich sauce made from rehydrated dried porcini mushrooms (Boletus edulis), heavy cream, butter, garlic, and Parmesan cheese, finished with fresh parsley and black pepper. The dish originates from northern Italian culinary tradition, where porcini mushrooms are prized for their deep, earthy, umami-rich flavour. The sauce is prepared by sautéing rehydrated porcini in butter and garlic, deglazing with the porcini soaking liquid, then reducing with heavy cream to create a velvety, intensely flavoured coating. The texture is silky and indulgent, with the pasta providing a firm al-dente bite against the creamy sauce. Nutritionally, porcini cream pasta is energy-dense at approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with a significant fat contribution (~10 g/100g, predominantly saturated from cream and butter) and moderate carbohydrate (~22 g/100g). The glycemic index of the pasta base is low (~45), but the glycemic load per standard restaurant serving (~350 g) is high (~35), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is a source of B vitamins from enriched pasta and provides modest calcium and iron. It is not suitable for vegans or those with gluten or dairy allergies. In Singapore, this dish is served at Western and Italian-style casual dining restaurants and café-bistros such as Flavours by Saute.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for porcini cream pasta exists in any approved primary regional or global nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked enriched pasta and a cream sauce component. GI is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white pasta cooked al dente as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite (cooked enriched pasta + cream sauce); no exact approved-source entry exists for porcini cream pasta. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (white pasta, al dente); no direct GI measurement for porcini cream pasta was found. 3. Multilingual local_names: Chinese simplified and traditional names provided (牛肝菌奶油意大利面 / 牛肝菌奶油意大利麵). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are null because this is an Italian dish served in a global cuisine context in Singapore, not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (130.0), calcium_mg (55.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (38.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (62.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.07), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.1), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (95.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (14.0), water_g (58.0) are all estimated from USDA composite; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates are available from the composite source. 5. is_halal set to false as the dish may contain wine in some preparations and halal certification is not confirmed for the source restaurant. 6. Price data from field survey data (SGD 20.30) is preserved as context but not used as a nutritional source.
